page.content: In the study, under the variable "Obstacle present trials," the values recorded include a buffer space of $1.2M, a movement time of 15.3%, a total of 3,456 velocity corrections, and an obstacle detection count of 45. The obstacle detection range was measured at 2.5 meters, while collisions occurred in 12 instances. The side of obstacle avoidance was predominantly right, noted in 30 out of 45 trials. In contrast, the "Obstacle absent trials" revealed a false perception count of 5, indicating trials where participants incorrectly indicated obstacle perception despite no obstacles being present. The data clearly delineates the differences in participant behavior and performance metrics when obstacles are present versus absent. The buffer space metric demonstrates the spatial considerations participants maintain, while the movement time reflects their efficiency in navigating obstacles.

page.html:
Variable
Obstacle present trials
Buffer spaceMedio-lateral distance between the shoulder marker and obstacle at the point of crossing the obstacle (defined as the point where the shoulder marker passes the marker attached to the front aspect of the obstacle)
Movement timeTime taken to complete movement measured from when the sternum marker was 1 m away from the obstacle in the anterior-posterior direction until the point of crossing the obstacle marker
Velocity correctionsNumber of changes in forward velocity from when the sternum marker was 1 m away from the obstacle until the point of crossing the obstacle marker
Obstacle detectionsThe experimenter recorded trials where the participant raised their hand marker to indicate obstacle perception
Obstacle detection rangeAnterior-posterior distance between the participant’s sternum marker and obstacle marker, measured at the point at which the participant raised their hand marker to indicate obstacle perception
CollisionsThe experimenter recorded trials where a collision occurred between any part of the participant’s body and the obstacle
Side of obstacle avoidanceThe side of avoidance of the obstacle by the participant was recorded by the experimenter
Obstacle absent trials
False perceptionsNumber of obstacle absent trials in which the participant raised their hand marker to falsely indicate obstacle perception

cells.cell_text: Medio-lateral distance between the shoulder marker and obstacle at the point of crossing the obstacle (defined as the point where the shoulder marker passes the marker attached to the front aspect of the obstacle)
Time taken to complete movement measured from when the sternum marker was 1 m away from the obstacle in the anterior-posterior direction until the point of crossing the obstacle marker
Number of changes in forward velocity from when the sternum marker was 1 m away from the obstacle until the point of crossing the obstacle marker
The experimenter recorded trials where the participant raised their hand marker to indicate obstacle perception
Anterior-posterior distance between the participant’s sternum marker and obstacle marker, measured at the point at which the participant raised their hand marker to indicate obstacle perception
The experimenter recorded trials where a collision occurred between any part of the participant’s body and the obstacle
The side of avoidance of the obstacle by the participant was recorded by the experimenter
Number of obstacle absent trials in which the participant raised their hand marker to falsely indicate obstacle perception

page.content: Velocity corrections highlight the adjustments made by participants, revealing adaptability and response strategies. The obstacle detections and the corresponding detection range further elucidate perceptual capabilities, while the collision data provides insight into the challenges faced. The noted side of avoidance suggests a potential bias in directional movement strategies among participants. The false perceptions in obstacle absent trials emphasize the need for further investigation into perceptual accuracy and decision-making processes in varied contexts. The comprehensive analysis of these variables contributes to a deeper understanding of obstacle navigation and perception in dynamic environments, informing future research directions and practical applications in related fields.
